items.title: Layers & Neurons
items.description: Width, depth, and the neuron-as-unit model define the structural vocabulary used to describe MLP architectures — input/hidden/output roles and how many neurons sit in each tier.

items.title: ReLU & Variants
items.description: Hidden layers in modern MLPs almost always apply ReLU (or a variant) after each linear map; without such a nonlinearity, stacked layers would not create non-linear decision boundaries.

items.title: Applying Activations
items.description: Each hidden tier ends with an element-wise activation applied to the linear pre-activations; this is the step that bends features so the next layer can combine them into non-linear outputs.

items.title: Regression vs Classification
items.description: The MLP hidden stack is shared; the output layer width and activation follow the supervised task — K logits for multi-class, sigmoid or two logits for binary, one linear unit for regression.

items.title: Perceptron
items.description: A single perceptron is the one-layer special case; adding hidden layers and training the full stack is what turns the classical unit into a multilayer perceptron.

items.title: Universal Approximation
items.description: Once you understand why hidden layers enable non-linear boundaries, the universal approximation theorem explains how wide or deep MLPs can approximate continuous functions under stated conditions.
